notes: Round 6 research focused on very recent news (February-March 2026). Cross-referenced against fights.json (415 entries) and all previous new-fights-march2026 round files. Searched 13 priority states plus Colorado, Minnesota, Kansas, Oklahoma, Arizona, Delaware, New Jersey, Maryland, Alabama. Found 4 genuinely new entries not yet tracked plus 7 status updates for existing entries. Removed duplicates: Gray Summit MO (already tracked as Villa Ridge), Mercer County KY (already tracked), Caddo Parish moratorium defeat (already in Caddo-Bossier summary), Independence referendum (moved to updates).

new_entries.summary: The City of Hilliard filed a lawsuit to block Amazon/AEP Ohio's plan to install 228 natural gas-fed solid oxide fuel cells on a six-acre site to supply 73MW of power to Amazon's 142-acre data center campus. The city says it is the 'largest of its kind in North America.' Hilliard hired lawyers to challenge Amazon's air permit, which allows emissions of up to 1.45 million pounds of CO2 per day -- comparable to the output of 66,000 cars. The fuel cells were approved by the Ohio Power Siting Board (state level) under a new law, bypassing local control. The Ohio EPA approved the air permit in October 2025; Hilliard is appealing. A nearby elementary school heightens community concerns about pollutants. AEP Ohio hopes to begin fuel cell construction in fall 2026.

new_entries.jurisdiction: St. Louis (Board of Aldermen moratorium defeat)
new_entries.summary: St. Louis Board of Aldermen voted down a proposal by Aldermen Michael Browning and Alisha Sonnier to suspend permit approvals for new data centers while the city completed new zoning regulations. More than half the board voted against even introducing the bill on the last day for new legislation. The defeat came despite hours of public testimony from residents opposing data center expansion and a Change.org petition with 12,300+ signatures. The St. Louis Planning Commission is separately developing restrictive zoning rules that would limit data centers to certain districts and impose operating requirements. Three heavily attended town halls produced massive public pushback. Breathe Better STL and other groups continue pushing for stronger regulations. This is a distinct legislative action from the existing St. Louis Armory data center fight (Resolution 111).

Three northeast Wisconsin towns -- Two Creeks, Two Rivers, and Mishicot -- passed resolutions calling on Manitowoc County to place a one-year moratorium on new data center permits and construction, with an option to extend for six months. The resolutions were prompted by Texas-based Cloverleaf Infrastructure approaching local landowners with offers to purchase farmland for a data center. The area is targeted due to its proximity to the Point Beach nuclear power plant. Farmers are concerned about losing agricultural land and residents worry about changes to the rural character. The resolutions go to the Manitowoc County Planning and Park Commission, then to the full county board for consideration.

West Rockhill Township supervisors advanced a data center regulation ordinance, nearly identical to one being considered in neighboring East Rockhill Township. The ordinance restricts data center construction to industrial districts and includes building structure requirements, accessory use rules, and equipment regulations. Both Bucks County townships are preemptively establishing data center zoning frameworks in advance of potential applications, following a model ordinance published by PennFuture. The township has not yet received any formal data center proposals. The ordinance must be publicly advertised and reviewed by township and county planning commissions before final approval.

updates.old_snippet: Despite overwhelming opposition, the Independence City Council voted on March 2, 2026 to approve the project
updates.new_value: After council approved the project 5-2 on March 2, residents launched a referendum petition drive on March 3 requiring 3,700 signatures from registered voters within 30 days to force a public vote. Organizers hope to place the question on the August 2026 ballot. The petition could coincide with April 7 municipal elections. Good Jobs First sent an open letter questioning the deal's terms.

updates.notes: Moratorium extended unanimously to June 5, 2026 at March 3 meeting. Commission voted to send text amendment proposals back to planning commission for revision. Considering Level 3 industrial threshold requirement (special use permit) for all data centers.

updates.old_snippet: The Board of Aldermen passed Resolution 111
updates.new_value: On Feb 13, 2026 aldermen voted down a separate attempt by Aldermen Browning and Sonnier to suspend data center permits pending new zoning rules. More than half the board voted against even introducing the bill. Meanwhile, St. Louis Planning Commission is developing restrictive new zoning rules; a public hearing on the Midtown Armory data center is set for March 2026.

updates.notes: Sedgwick County Commission unanimously extended data center permit moratorium to June 11, 2026. State Senator Chase Blasi (Senate majority leader) asked for a three-year moratorium. Town hall scheduled for March 31. Planning department studying zoning protocols.

updates.old_snippet: Hood County commissioners voted down the moratorium
updates.new_value: Hood County commissioners rejected the moratorium a second time on Feb 25, 2026, in a 3-2 vote. Instead, they voted to seek an opinion from TX Attorney General Ken Paxton on whether counties have authority to enact development moratoriums. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt warned counties lack constitutional authority for moratoriums. Residents called for commissioner resignations after the second defeat.

updates.old_snippet: Part of a broader fight in Hays County
updates.new_value: Hays County Judge Ruben Becerra proposed a moratorium on permits for large industrial water users (>20,000 gal/day) but the Commissioners Court tabled it on Feb 25 after county attorneys warned it would trigger a lawsuit the county would lose. State Sen. Bettencourt warned counties lack authority for moratoriums. Water advocates have identified 5 potential data center developments in the region and continue organizing.

updates.notes: Wilmington City Council approved annexation and rezoning of ~545 acres for AWS despite continued opposition. However, separate site-plan, utility, and tax-incentive reviews are still required before construction. The site plan was tabled after city staff identified missing details on water service demands, cooling methods, noise mitigation, and pollution -- which AWS called 'proprietary information.'
